perturbation.original_text: Upon termination of membership, Receiving Party will immediately return to the Society all records, notes and other written, printed or tangible materi al in its possession, including CDs and DVDs pertaining to Confidential In formation. Confidential Information stored electronically in computers or any other electronic devices will be deleted upon membership termination.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon termination of membership, Receiving Party will return to the Society all records, notes and other written, printed or tangible materi al in its possession, including CDs and DVDs pertaining to Confidential In formation within 90 days of the termination date. Confidential Information stored electronically in computers or any other electronic devices will be deleted within 90 days of membership termination.
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the return and deletion timeframe to 90 days contradicts the immediate action required by privacy laws in Canada upon termination of an agreement where personal information is involved.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act (PIPEDA), Principles 4.5 and 4.5.3
perturbation.law_explanation: PIPEDA requires organizations to destroy or erase personal information when it is no longer needed for the purpose for which it was collected. While it doesn't specify 'immediately,' a 90-day delay is likely unreasonable and contradicts the spirit and intent of minimizing retention, particularly after termination of membership which revokes the initial purpose of information collection.

perturbation.justification: The original contract requires immediate return and deletion of confidential information upon termination of membership. The changed text allows for a 90-day delay, which contradicts the principle of minimizing retention, especially after termination. The law explanation states that PIPEDA requires organizations to destroy or erase personal information when it is no longer needed, and a 90-day delay is likely unreasonable. While the provided snippets do not explicitly mention 'immediate' action, the law explanation, based on the general intent of PIPEDA, indicates that the 90-day delay creates a contradiction with the spirit of the law.

perturbation.original_text: Receiving Party shall hold and maintain the Confidential Information in st rictest confidence for the sole and exclusive purposes of the Society. Receiving Party shall not use any Confidential Information for their own benefit or permit the use of Confidential Information by others for their benefit or to the detriment of the Society or its good name.
perturbation.changed_text: Receiving Party shall hold and maintain the Confidential Information in st rictest confidence for the sole and exclusive purposes of the Society, unless compelled by law or legal process. Receiving Party shall not use any Confidential Information for their own benefit or permit the use of Confidential Information by others for their benefit or to the detriment of the Society or its good name.
perturbation.explanation: Adding "unless compelled by law or legal process" without specifying required notification to the Society directly contradicts legal obligations to inform affected parties when their data is disclosed under legal compulsion, potentially violating privacy regulations in Canada.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act (PIPEDA), Section 7(3)(c)
perturbation.law_explanation: While PIPEDA Section 7(3)(c) allows disclosure required by law, it doesn't negate the obligation to inform the affected individuals/organization (in this case, the Society) about the disclosure, especially if feasible. By not including a notification requirement, the modified clause creates a potential violation of transparency and accountability principles under PIPEDA.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Privacy Act Skip to main content Skip to "About this site" Skip to section menu Marginal note: Use of personal information 7 Â Personal information under the control of a government institution shall not, without the consent of the individual to whom it relates, be used by the institution except (a) Â for the purpose for which the information was obtained or compiled by the institution or for a use consistent with that purpose; or (b) Â for a purpose for which the information may be disclosed to the institution under subsection 8(2). 1980-81-82-83, c. 111, Sch. II â7â Table of Contents 1 ... 8 9 ... 19 20 ... 34 35 ... 51 52 ... 66 66.1 ... 74 75 ... 77 SCHEDULE : Government Institutions Related Provisions Amendments not in force Previous Amendments ï»¿ Date modified: 2025-07-08 ï»¿
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a legal contradiction by adding "unless compelled by law or legal process" without requiring notification to the Society. The Law Explanation states that while PIPEDA Section 7(3)(c) allows disclosure required by law, it doesn't negate the obligation to inform the affected organization about the disclosure. Scraped Law Snippet 1, although pertaining to the Privacy Act, highlights the principle that personal information under the control of a government institution shall not be used without consent, reinforcing the need for transparency and accountability when such information is disclosed under legal compulsion. Therefore, the modified clause potentially violates transparency and accountability principles, leading to a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The nondisclosure provision of this Agreement shall survive the termination of this Agreement and Receiving Party’s duty to hold Confidential Information in confidence shall remain in effect indefinitely.
perturbation.changed_text: The nondisclosure provision of this Agreement shall survive the termination of this Agreement and Receiving Party’s duty to hold Confidential Information in confidence shall remain in effect for 10 years.
perturbation.explanation: Limiting the non-disclosure provision to 10 years may be insufficient to protect trade secrets or other confidential information under Canadian law, where the reasonable duration of confidentiality can extend beyond this period depending on the information's nature and the industry.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Canadian law regarding trade secrets and confidential information
perturbation.law_citation: Common Law principles regarding trade secrets in Canada
perturbation.law_explanation: While there's no single statute defining the duration of trade secret protection in Canada, common law principles dictate that protection lasts as long as the information remains confidential and provides a competitive advantage. A fixed 10-year period might be insufficient to protect information with a longer useful life, potentially weakening the Society's legal recourse against misappropriation of trade secrets.

perturbation.justification: The modified text changes the duration of the non-disclosure provision from 'indefinitely' to '10 years'. The Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation suggest that this fixed period may be insufficient to protect trade secrets under Canadian common law, where protection lasts as long as the information remains confidential and provides a competitive advantage. While the scraped snippets are not directly relevant to Canadian law or trade secrets, the provided explanations highlight a potential contradiction, suggesting the modified contract could weaken legal recourse against misappropriation. The change introduces a limitation that might not adequately protect confidential information, potentially contradicting the principles of trade secret law that aim to protect information as long as it remains confidential and valuable.

perturbation.original_text: If proven Improper Disclosure sha ll take place, the Receiving Party’s membership in the Society shall be terminated immediately. Depending on circumstances, there may also be recourse to the courts and criminal or civil action, or both, may be initiated by the Society or by injured parties.
perturbation.changed_text: If Improper Disclosure is suspected, the Receiving Party’s membership in the Society shall be terminated immediately. Depending on circumstances, there may also be recourse to the courts and criminal or civil action, or both, may be initiated by the Society or by injured parties.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "proven Improper Disclosure" to "suspected" and allowing immediate termination contradicts principles of natural justice and procedural fairness required in Canada. Membership termination based on suspicion without due process violates the right to a fair hearing.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Principles of Natural Justice and Procedural Fairness under Canadian Law
perturbation.law_citation: Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, Section 7
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 7 of the Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms guarantees the right to life, liberty and security of the person, and the right not to be deprived thereof except in accordance with the principles of fundamental justice. Terminating membership based solely on suspicion without affording the member a fair opportunity to be heard and present a defense violates these principles of natural justice and procedural fairness, potentially leading to legal challenges.

perturbation.justification: The change from "proven Improper Disclosure" to "suspected" significantly lowers the standard for membership termination, contradicting principles of natural justice and procedural fairness, as it allows termination based on mere suspicion without due process. The LLM Law explanation states this violates Section 7 of the Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms. While Scraped Law Snippet 2 only indicates a file not found error, the Law Explanation provided by the LLM is strong enough to justify the existence of a legal contradiction.
